questions.title: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questions.text: A patient with cirrhosis develops ascites. What is the primary pathophysiological mechanism that contributes to the development of ascites in this patient?

choices.text: Increased oncotic pressure in the capillaries
Portal hypertension and decreased serum albumin

questions.rationale: Ascites in cirrhosis is primarily caused by portal hypertension, which increases hydrostatic pressure in the capillaries, and decreased serum albumin, which reduces oncotic pressure. These two factors lead to fluid leaking from the capillaries into the peritoneal cavity. Aldosterone levels are actually increased, renal excretion of sodium is decreased, and oncotic pressure is decreased.

questions.title: Nutrition
questions.text: A patient with cirrhosis is being discharged home. Which dietary modification is most important for the nurse to emphasize to the patient and their family?

choices.text: Increase protein intake significantly to promote liver regeneration
Restrict sodium intake to manage fluid retention
Consume a high-fat diet for energy
Eliminate all sugar from the diet to prevent further liver damage

questions.rationale: Sodium restriction is crucial in managing fluid retention (ascites and edema) associated with cirrhosis. While adequate protein intake is important, it needs to be balanced and individualized. High-fat diets can be difficult for the liver to process. While minimizing excessive sugar intake is helpful for overall health, sodium restriction is the priority in managing cirrhosis-related complications.

questions.title: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questions.text: A nurse is caring for a patient with cirrhosis who has developed hepatic encephalopathy. Which laboratory finding would the nurse expect to see elevated?
questions.rationale: Hepatic encephalopathy is caused by the liver's inability to detoxify ammonia. Elevated serum ammonia levels are a key indicator of hepatic encephalopathy. Serum creatinine reflects kidney function. Serum albumin is usually decreased in cirrhosis. Serum potassium may fluctuate but is not the primary indicator of encephalopathy.

questions.title: Pharmacology
questions.text: A patient with cirrhosis and ascites is prescribed spironolactone. What is the primary reason for using spironolactone in this patient?

choices.text: To increase potassium excretion and prevent hyperkalemia
To promote sodium and water excretion while sparing potassium
To lower blood pressure and reduce portal hypertension
To improve liver function and promote healing

questions.rationale: Spironolactone is a potassium-sparing diuretic that is commonly used in cirrhosis to promote sodium and water excretion, thereby reducing ascites, while minimizing the risk of hypokalemia. It also blocks the effects of aldosterone which is often elevated in cirrhosis. It is not used primarily to lower blood pressure or improve liver function.

questions.title: Assessment
questions.text: Which physical assessment finding is most indicative of portal hypertension in a patient with cirrhosis?
questions.rationale: Portal hypertension is increased pressure in the portal venous system. This leads to ascites (fluid accumulation in the abdomen) and splenomegaly (enlargement of the spleen) due to backflow of blood. Peripheral edema can occur due to low albumin, and jaundice and pruritus are due to impaired liver function, but these are not as directly indicative of portal hypertension as ascites and splenomegaly.

questions.title: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questions.text: A patient with cirrhosis is undergoing a paracentesis. What is the most important nursing action during this procedure?

choices.text: Administering a bolus of intravenous fluids after the procedure
Monitoring the patient for hypotension and signs of hypovolemia
Encouraging the patient to ambulate immediately after the procedure
Applying a warm compress to the insertion site

questions.rationale: Paracentesis involves removing fluid from the peritoneal cavity. Rapid removal of large amounts of fluid can lead to hypotension and hypovolemia due to fluid shifts. Monitoring for these signs is crucial. IV fluids are sometimes given during or after, but vigilant monitoring is the priority. Ambulation should be gradual and monitored. A sterile dressing is applied, not a warm compress.

questions.title: Pharmacology
questions.text: Which medication is commonly used to treat hepatic encephalopathy by reducing ammonia levels?
questions.rationale: Lactulose is a synthetic disaccharide that is used to treat hepatic encephalopathy. It works by acidifying the colon, which converts ammonia (NH3) to ammonium (NH4+), which is then trapped in the colon and excreted in the feces. Furosemide is a diuretic. Ondansetron is an antiemetic. Metoprolol can be used for portal hypertension but does not directly reduce ammonia.

questions.title: Patient Education
questions.text: A patient with cirrhosis asks the nurse about the cause of their jaundice. Which explanation is most accurate?

choices.text: "It's due to an increase in red blood cell production."
"It's caused by the liver's inability to process bilirubin."
"It's a result of an infection in your gallbladder."
"It's due to a decrease in the amount of iron in your blood."

questions.rationale: Jaundice in cirrhosis is caused by the liver's impaired ability to conjugate and excrete bilirubin, a byproduct of red blood cell breakdown. This leads to a buildup of bilirubin in the blood, causing the yellow discoloration of the skin and eyes. Jaundice is not directly related to red blood cell production, gallbladder infection, or iron levels.

questions.title: Complications
questions.text: A patient with cirrhosis develops esophageal varices. What is the most life-threatening risk associated with esophageal varices?
questions.rationale: Esophageal varices are dilated veins in the esophagus that are prone to rupture and cause massive gastrointestinal bleeding. This bleeding can be life-threatening. While the other options can occur, they are not as immediately dangerous as hemorrhage.

questions.title: Nursing Interventions
questions.text: What nursing intervention is most important to implement for a patient with cirrhosis who has ascites?

choices.text: Limit fluid intake to 3 liters per day
Elevate the head of the bed to 30 degrees only

questions.rationale: Measuring abdominal girth daily is important to monitor the progression or resolution of ascites. A low-sodium diet is essential. Fluid restriction is often necessary but is individualized. Elevating the head of the bed assists with breathing; a higher elevation (semi-Fowler's or Fowler's) may be beneficial.

questions.title: Cirrhosis & Assessment
questions.text: When assessing a client with cirrhosis, the nurse palpates the abdomen. Which finding is most likely related to the liver?
questions.rationale: In cirrhosis, the liver typically becomes hard and nodular due to the formation of scar tissue. A soft and non-tender liver is normal, but not typical in cirrhosis. Bowel sounds are related to the intestines, not the liver directly.

questions.title: Cirrhosis & Dietary Management
questions.text: A client with cirrhosis is discussing their diet with the nurse. Which food choice indicates a need for further teaching?
questions.rationale: Canned soup is typically high in sodium, which should be restricted in clients with cirrhosis to manage ascites and edema. Fresh fruit, grilled chicken, and steamed vegetables are generally appropriate choices.

questions.title: Cirrhosis & Collaborative Care
questions.text: A client with cirrhosis is scheduled for a transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt (TIPS) procedure. The nurse understands that the primary goal of this procedure is to:
questions.rationale: The TIPS procedure creates a channel between the portal vein and the hepatic vein, which reduces portal hypertension and its complications, such as esophageal varices and ascites. It does not directly improve liver function, remove fluid from the abdomen (though it can help), or decrease ammonia levels (and can sometimes worsen hepatic encephalopathy).

questions.title: Cirrhosis & Respiratory Complications
questions.text: Which nursing intervention is most important for preventing pneumonia in a client with cirrhosis and ascites?

choices.text: Encouraging deep breathing and coughing exercises

questions.rationale: Encouraging deep breathing and coughing exercises helps to prevent pneumonia by promoting lung expansion and clearing secretions. Ascites can put pressure on the diaphragm, making it difficult to breathe deeply. Restricting fluid intake is for managing ascites, not preventing pneumonia. Prophylactic antibiotics are not routinely given. Limited activity can worsen respiratory function.

questions.title: Cirrhosis & Complications
questions.text: Which assessment findings would support a diagnosis of fluid volume excess in a client with cirrhosis?

choices.text: Pitting edema in the lower extremities

questions.rationale: Fluid volume excess in cirrhosis is evidenced by increased abdominal girth (ascites) and pitting edema. Tachycardia (increased heart rate) is more common with fluid overload. Dry mucous membranes indicate fluid volume deficit.
